Claude.notes: Research is dense and the three-layer taxonomy is a clean organizing frame, but the module reads as a well-written briefing rather than a narrative lesson. The Microsoft / OpenAI opening creates the right problem, then the story basically ends — each lesson is 85–95% expository concept. No protagonist threads L1 to L4. The bigger Applied-Outcome issue is the quiz: all five questions in L1 test recall of cited statistics, and the same pattern repeats across the other lessons. For Advanced learners that should be where judgment is exercised.
Claude.topIssue: Quizzes across all four lessons test recall only; the learner never has to apply, evaluate, or judge between competing options.

Gemini.notes: Technical content is mostly accurate. EU AI Act timing, Gebru/Stochastic Parrots, NeurIPS 2023 stats, Hinton's 2023 departure, and the Anthropic prompt-engineer range all check out. Two flags: the L2 framing credits RLHF specifically to Christiano and Amodei, which understates the broader OpenAI/DeepMind contributions, and the three-layer model is presented as cleanly disjoint when in practice the boundaries are highly permeable. Both could leave learners with a tidier mental model than the field actually supports.
Gemini.topIssue: RLHF attribution is too narrow — crediting Christiano and Amodei alone elides that RLHF was developed by a larger team across OpenAI and DeepMind over several papers.

Perplexity.notes: The headline labour-market data (Stanford AI Index 2024, LinkedIn 2024, Levels.fyi 2024) is current, and the EU AI Act and US Executive Order references are correctly dated. But the load-bearing case examples are increasingly old: Spotify Discover Weekly (2015), Buolamwini's MIT work (2016), the Gebru firing (2020), and Indeed's 10,000-posting analysis (2023) all serve as anchors. The lab is still executable in 2026, but a learner reading this in mid-2026 would expect at least one 2025-era case (post-GPT-4o frontier-lab hiring, the safety-team headcount surge, the agent-engineer role emergence).
Perplexity.topIssue: Anchor cases are 3–11 years old; the module has no 2025–26 examples to ground a current learner in today's AI labour market.

Claude.notes: The four-role taxonomy (ML engineer / data scientist / research scientist / MLOps) lands cleanly and the case selection — Transformer (2017), Netflix Prize, AlphaFold 2, Uber Michelangelo — does the heavy lifting. The story in each lesson is short but functional: it sets up the problem the concept then solves. Where the module loses ground is character continuity (each lesson resets to a new institutional cast) and quiz mix (leans recall in L3 and L4, where 'why a PhD' and 'feature store' could be sharper judgment items).
Claude.topIssue: Stories function as case-study anchors rather than narrative arcs; no recurring characters across L1–L4, capping engagement.

Gemini.notes: The role descriptions and salary ranges are credible. Two technical definitions slipped, though: 'model drift' is given as the umbrella for what the industry usually splits into data drift, prediction drift, and concept drift, and 'quantization' is sold as 'minimal accuracy loss' without acknowledging it can be 0.1–2% with real safety implications in high-stakes domains. AlphaFold 2 / CASP14 / NeurIPS 2012 facts are all stated correctly.
Gemini.topIssue: Model drift definition conflates three distinct phenomena (data, prediction, concept drift); quantization framed as 'minimal accuracy loss' is unquantified and risky for safety-critical contexts.

Perplexity.notes: Salary bands (2024 US market) and the Google Brain → DeepMind merger reference are current. Otherwise the case load is heavily 2015–2020: Netflix Prize, Airbnb photo discovery, Uber Michelangelo, AlphaFold 2. By mid-2026 a learner picking a technical track expects exposure to LLM-era infrastructure cases — vLLM, distributed RLHF training, eval infra at frontier labs — and that texture is absent.
Perplexity.topIssue: Cases cluster on 2015–2020 inflection points; the module gives no exposure to 2024–25 LLM-era technical roles or infrastructure.

Claude.notes: Best-structured module in the course so far. The November-2023 OpenAI-board crisis story creates a real governance vacuum the four-level concept then maps. Quizzes mix recall with applied judgment — the Canada/Germany hiring-tool jurisdictional question and the 'Brussels Effect' item both force the learner to reason. The labs (Canada/Germany compliance, transition-into-governance career, audit kickoff, five-market launch) are all chat-executable and scenario-grounded. Narrative density still runs low for Advanced, and there are no recurring characters across lessons.
Claude.topIssue: Concept-first design is excellent but narrative density (~15–25%) and zero character continuity across L1–L4 leave the module emotionally flat for a learner who isn't already motivated.

Gemini.notes: Regulatory specifics are tight throughout. The EU AI Act risk tiers, OMB M-24-10, NIST AI RMF (Jan 2023), the Chouldechova/Kleinberg impossibility result, NYC Local Law 144's effective date, China's 'core socialist values' compliance clause, the October-2022/October-2023 BIS export controls on A100/H100, and the Council of Europe Framework Convention being binding international AI law are all stated correctly. The COMPAS summary is slightly simplified — it elides Northpointe's calibration-defence — but the rest of the L3 material correctly flags that fairness definitions are mutually exclusive.
Gemini.topIssue: COMPAS framing is one notch too clean — Northpointe's calibration counter-argument should be introduced earlier rather than as a footnote, since it's the load-bearing example for the impossibility result.

Perplexity.notes: Currency is the strongest in the course. EU AI Act (March 2024), Seoul AI Safety Summit (May 2024), the UN High-Level Advisory Body report (September 2024), and the Council of Europe Framework Convention (September 2024) are all within their freshness window for a 2026 reader. The Brazilian AI Bill is described as 'in legislative process as of 2024' — by mid-2026 that needs a status check. The L4 lab (five-market launch including Brazil) actively asks learners to grapple with an unsettled jurisdiction, which mitigates the risk somewhat.
Perplexity.topIssue: Brazilian AI Bill described as 'in legislative process as of 2024' — by mid-2026 the status should be verified and the lesson refreshed.

Claude.notes: Jason Allen's Colorado State Fair entry is the best story-as-problem in the course — it sets up the authorship question the concept then redefines as a transferable design discipline. Labs (mood-board for Verdant, fintech AI trust patterns, B2B content strategy) are concrete and immediately executable. The module's main narrative weakness is that L1's protagonist doesn't reappear; L2–L4 reset to organisations and case studies, so there's no through-line.
Claude.topIssue: L1's Jason Allen is the strongest character beat in the course but he disappears after the opening; each subsequent lesson resets to a new cast, breaking continuity.

Gemini.notes: Latent diffusion, seed control, negative prompts, --sref/--cref are all defined correctly. Two oversimplifications: stem-separation tools (Lalal.ai, Moises) are framed as fitting into 'sample-clearance workflows' when they only help identify samples — they don't grant rights. And the Figma 'Make Designs' / Apple Weather similarity is described as a discrete incident, when the broader story is systemic training-data risk across every UI design tool. Adobe Firefly's 'licensed and public-domain only' claim is reported correctly per Adobe's stated position.
Gemini.topIssue: Stem separation framed as solving sample-clearance, and Figma 'Make Designs' framed as a one-off incident rather than a systemic training-data risk — both understate the actual technical/legal posture.

Perplexity.notes: The module reads as 'early-to-mid 2024' content. By June 2026 several anchor stories have either resolved or shifted shape: the UMG/Sony/Warner v. Suno/Udio litigation has progressed, the NO FAKES Act has either passed or stalled, the Figma 'Make Designs' feature has been retooled, and the WGA contract is now mid-cycle. The L3 lab specifically asks learners to advise on Suno/Udio licensing — without a 2025–26 refresh that advice is built on a moving legal floor. The core tools (Midjourney, Firefly, Dovetail) are still current.
Perplexity.topIssue: Suno/Udio litigation and NO FAKES Act presented as live, in-flight; by mid-2026 these legal questions have shifted, leaving the L3 lab built on a stale legal floor.

Claude.notes: The strongest module in the course. The three-pass reading method, the GLUE/SuperGLUE saturation arc, the Google Duplex case as a worked example of 'what's missing' is the limitations section, and the arXiv-flood triage problem are all teach-by-example moves that genuinely change how a learner reads papers. Quizzes mix recall with judgment (e.g., 'which raises the most scrutiny in a results table'). Capability delta is unusually concrete: 'read and critically evaluate a published AI paper.' Only weak axis is narrative density — stories are brief hooks rather than arcs.
Claude.topIssue: Story density still runs low (~10–20% per lesson) — even strong opening hooks like the Transformer narrative are over within a paragraph, leaving the bulk of each lesson as exposition.

Gemini.notes: Every technical claim I checked holds up. BLEU as n-gram surface overlap, ablation studies, benchmark saturation on ImageNet/GLUE/SuperGLUE, the Schaeffer et al. (2023) 'emergent abilities as metric artefact' critique, NeurIPS's Broader Impacts requirement since 2021, the 25–30% Papers With Code reproducibility failure rate, ICLR's OpenReview public-review model, and the AlphaFold-2 timeline (CASP14 Nov 2020 → Nature July 2021) are all stated correctly. Definitions of preprint, ablation, SOTA, peer review (2–5 reviewers, single rebuttal) are precise.
Gemini.topIssue: No technical errors flagged; module is the cleanest in the course. Only future-proofing note: post-Chinchilla scaling papers (2023–24) and METR ARA evaluations could enrich L3.

Perplexity.notes: Citations land squarely in the 2012–2023 window with no obviously dated examples held up as 'current practice.' The arXiv cs.LG submission volume (~6,000 in Jan 2023) is the most temporally fragile data point and would benefit from a 2025–26 refresh — submission volumes have continued to grow. Tracking-tools lesson (Papers With Code, Semantic Scholar, Hugging Face Daily Papers, Connected Papers) is all still active. METR's ARA evaluation work and RewardBench-style frontier-model benchmarks would round out the picture for a 2026 reader.
Perplexity.topIssue: Excellent through early 2023 but the module should refresh arXiv volume stats and add at least one 2024–25 evaluation reference (METR ARA, RewardBench, or post-Chinchilla scaling) for a 2026 learner.

Claude.notes: Capability delta is the highest in the course — the learner walks out with a personalised 12-month plan, a skill-gap audit, a portfolio project design, and an outreach strategy, all built across four chat labs. Aakash Nain and Chip Huyen are the strongest individual case anchors in the whole course. Quiz still leans recall in 3 of 4 lessons (the system-design item in L4 is the standout judgment question). Narrative density edges up to ~25% which is closer to Advanced target.
Claude.topIssue: Quiz design remains recall-heavy in 3 of 4 lessons — given how scenario-rich the labs are, the quizzes should be testing the same applied judgment.

Gemini.notes: Career-evidence citations are real: O'Reilly 2023 communication-gap finding, MLOps.community 2023 hiring-manager survey, Pragmatic Engineer 2023 referral analysis, Josh Doody 2023 negotiation case studies, Andrew Ng Coursera enrolments. The L1 claim that 'linear algebra ranked #1 (78%) in the 2022 DeepLearning.AI survey' is plausible but single-source — and a learner could easily over-index on math foundations relative to current 2026 hiring practice. The 'system design over LeetCode' guidance (from Chip Huyen's work) is correct and counter-intuitive in a useful way.
Gemini.topIssue: L1's 'linear algebra #1 at 78%' claim is single-sourced (DeepLearning.AI 2022) and could mislead learners about relative skill weightings in 2026 hiring.

Perplexity.notes: The four-pillar framing (math, programming, domain, communication) is anchored in 2022–2023 surveys and 2017–2021 case studies (fast.ai, IDx-DR, AlphaFold). For a learner reading this in mid-2026, the LLM/agent shift is conspicuously underweighted — there's no real treatment of prompt engineering as a skill, LLM fine-tuning, RAG pipelines, agentic system design, evals, or the 'Applied AI Engineer' role that dominates 2025–26 hiring. Bootcamp economics from the 2022 SwitchUp audit ($78k start, 4.5 months) pre-date the bootcamp market contraction, and the Levels.fyi 2023 salary bands pre-date the post-2023 equity-grant compression. The capability delta is excellent if the roadmap targets the right destination — but the destination it points at is closer to 2022's AI job market than 2026's.
Perplexity.topIssue: Roadmap underweights LLM, agent, and prompt-engineering tracks that dominate 2025–26 AI hiring; bootcamp data (2022 SwitchUp) and salary bands (Levels.fyi 2023) are stale.
